sourceMovie.title: Breathless
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Breathless for the same reason it broke through in 1960: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a crime rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Jean-Luc Godard keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The drama and crime blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Le Samouraï
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Breathless clicked for you, Le Samouraï (1967) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and crime, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Frantic
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Breathless clicked for you, Frantic (1988) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and crime, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Pierrot le Fou
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Breathless clicked for you, Pierrot le Fou (1965) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and crime, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Breathless?
faq.answer: Try Le Samouraï, Frantic, Pierrot le Fou—each shares DNA with Breathless while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Breathless?
faq.answer: Breathless (1960) is best described as Drama, Crime. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Breathless worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
